persona.communication_style: Prefers quiet, considered, instrument-first communication that treats sound as memory and texture before melody or arrangement. Responds best to language about specific hardware (Buchla Music Easel, Roland MC-202, Make Noise, modular eurorack), signal degradation, tape, room sound, and the emotional residue a piece leaves behind, and to examples grounded in real solo composition or live touring work. Dislikes feature-list pitches, anything that abstracts away the physical instrument, and tools that mistake gloss for depth.
persona.name: Alessandro Cortini

occupation.description: Alessandro Cortini is an Italian composer, synthesist, and performer whose solo work (Forse, Sonno, Risveglio, Avanti, Volume Massimo, Scuro Chiaro) explores memory, nostalgia, and emotional weight through a tightly chosen palette — most famously the Roland MC-202, Buchla Music Easel, and a small set of eurorack modules. He is a long-time member of Nine Inch Nails' live band and How to Destroy Angels, a frequent collaborator (Daniel Avery, Merzbow, Lawrence English, SPC ECO), and a co-designer with Make Noise on instruments like Strega. He evaluates tools through the lens of a solo composer-performer who works deeply with very few instruments: whether they have a strong character of their own, whether their imperfections and limitations are interesting, whether they translate from home studio to a quiet seated audience in a cathedral or theater, and whether they belong to a lineage worth extending.
occupation.organization: Independent solo artist (releases on Mute, Hospital Productions, Important Records, Point of Departure), long-time member of Nine Inch Nails live band, How to Destroy Angels, design collaborator with Make Noise (Strega and related instruments), and a wide collaborator network across ambient, industrial, and experimental scenes
occupation.title: Composer, synthesist, solo performer, touring band member (Nine Inch Nails), and instrument-design collaborator

other_facts: jobs_to_be_done: Build solo records around a small, deliberately constrained palette (often a single primary instrument) and let the limitation shape the work.
jobs_to_be_done: Translate personal memory — family, home, places, photographs — into sound that carries that emotional weight without literal narrative.
jobs_to_be_done: Perform solo shows in seated, listening-focused rooms (theaters, churches, cathedrals, art spaces) where dynamics and silence matter.
jobs_to_be_done: Hold down a demanding role in the Nine Inch Nails live band across long world tours while keeping the solo practice alive.
jobs_to_be_done: Collaborate with instrument designers (Make Noise / Strega) to shape tools whose character he will live with for years.
jobs_to_be_done: Use tape, signal degradation, and analog imperfection as compositional materials rather than as effects added on top.
jobs_to_be_done: Maintain a long, slow relationship with a few instruments rather than constantly acquiring new gear.
pains: Tools whose character is too neutral — they can do everything and therefore sound like nothing in particular.
pains: Workflows that hide the instrument behind menus, presets, or a laptop and break the intimate, tactile relationship he composes from.
pains: Pristine digital chains that strip away the noise, drift, and degradation his music depends on.
pains: Performance setups that don't survive a real touring schedule — temperature, transport, voltage, soundcheck constraints.
pains: Marketing that frames experimental and ambient work as background or wellness content rather than as composition.
pains: Collaboration tools that disrupt the slow, careful, instrument-led way he develops material.
pains: Anything that demands he abandon a long-trusted instrument to prove a new tool's value.
adoption_yes_if: The tool has its own strong sonic character, even — especially — when that character includes limitations.
adoption_yes_if: It rewards living with it: hours of patching, listening, and revisiting reveal more rather than less.
adoption_yes_if: It works well as part of a small, focused setup that can travel and be set up quickly in a quiet room.
adoption_yes_if: It treats noise, drift, tape behavior, and signal degradation as first-class materials.
adoption_yes_if: It integrates cleanly with the MC-202 / Music Easel / eurorack / tape signal world rather than demanding an in-the-box workflow.
adoption_yes_if: It is reliable enough for both intimate solo shows and the demands of a Nine Inch Nails tour.
adoption_yes_if: It respects an instrument-design lineage (Buchla, Roland, Make Noise) instead of imitating it superficially.
rejection_no_if: It is generic, characterless, or aesthetically interchangeable with any other tool in its category.
rejection_no_if: It forces a DAW-centric, grid-locked workflow that fights the way he actually composes from a single instrument.
rejection_no_if: It produces output that sounds clean in a way that erases the emotional residue he is after.
rejection_no_if: It introduces fragility into either the solo touring rig or the NIN live rig.
rejection_no_if: It collapses the distinction between solo composition and ambient background content.
rejection_no_if: It demands he learn a large surface area when the whole point of his practice is constraint.
rejection_no_if: It markets itself on novelty rather than on what it sounds like after a year of use.
anti_goals: He does not want a do-everything synth — depth comes from limitation.
anti_goals: He does not want pristine, denoised sound where degradation is the subject.
anti_goals: He does not want to hide instruments behind a laptop on stage.
anti_goals: He does not want production pressure to push solo work toward beats, length, or arrangement conventions he hasn't chosen.
anti_goals: He does not want collaboration tooling that speeds up a process that needs to be slow.
anti_goals: He does not want his solo work flattened into a wellness or background category.
anti_goals: He does not want to constantly chase new gear — the practice is staying with a few instruments long enough to hear them.

recognition_cues: Uses phrases such as “What does it sound like by itself?”, “Is there enough character to live with this for a year?”, “Does this hold up in a quiet room?”, “How does it travel?”, “What does the noise floor sound like?”, “Is the limitation interesting?”, “Does this remember anything?”, “Can I do a whole record on just this?”, “Does it sit next to the MC-202 or the Easel without fighting them?”, and “What does it feel like under the hands at 1 a.m.?”
